title: P0 control-plane death spiral: tp1 OOM (100MB free) + etcd DB bloat 1.2GB thrashing
description: ROOT CAUSE of all ingress outages (jme cloudflare, 8f0 tailscale). tp1 (192.168.68.107, ARM RK1 8GB) has ~100MB available RAM -> OOM-kills etcd/apiserver/cilium/coredns/kubelet (exit 137). etcd DB grew 900MB->1.2GB in ~30min, leader flapping tp1<->tp4, raft term 1747->1776 fast. Likely: apiserver flap -> etcd auto-compaction stalls -> history accumulates -> DB grows -> tp1 OOM -> more apiserver flap. KubePrism localhost:7445 returns EOF -> cilium-agent tp1/tp4 killed -> DNS+pod net break -> cloudflared crashloop -> CF tunnel down (Error 1033) + tailscale proxy dead. Trajectory: hits 2GB etcd quota -> NOSPACE read-only -> full outage in ~1-2h. FIX: etcd compact+defrag to halt growth, free/relieve tp1 memory, then networking recovers. Control-plane risk - confirm before destructive ops.
notes: tp1 RES breakdown: kube-apiserver 955MB, etcd 181MB RES (12GB VIRT=mmap, DB 1.2GB on disk), kubelet 98MB, plus ~30 workload pods (istio gateway envoy, cilium-envoy, coredns, bind9/named, metrics-server, VPA admission, longhorn-instance-manager, pyrra, cert-manager, node_exporter, frr, kube-proxy, pilot). tp1 is an 8GB ARM RK1 also acting as control-plane+etcd voter = oversubscribed -> OOM. AGGRAVATED by macintel01+rpi01 being cordoned (SchedulingDisabled) which removes capacity and concentrates workloads onto tp1/tp4/dell01/pc01. etcd DB 1.2GB IN-USE 100% (no fragmentation, defrag wont help) heading toward 2GB quota=NOSPACE. Two problems: (1) tp1 memory oversubscription, (2) etcd DB growth.

title: Tailscale ingress down - ts-istio-gateway proxy stuck on flapping macarm01
description: ts-istio-gateway-wsmws-0 (backs syscd-gateway tailnet device, all *.syscd.tech) and tailscale operator pods stuck Error/Terminating on macarm01. StatefulSet 0/1. Also kills the talos-default-ts kubectl context. macarm01 is a resource-starved Apple Silicon UTM VM that flaps NotReady (kubelet stops posting, apid TLS handshake times out ~2/3). In-guest talos reboot did not stick. Fix: stabilize/hard-restart macarm01 VM on Mac host; reschedule ts proxies to healthy node.

title: Cloudflare Tunnel down (Error 1033) - cloudflared crashlooping on macintel01, cannot resolve CoreDNS
description: cloudflared pod pinned to macintel01 (cross-site node, 118 restarts). DNS lookups to 10.96.0.10:53 i/o timeout -> crashloop -> tunnel disconnected -> all *.syscd.live return CF Error 1033 (obs, argocd, zigbee2mqtt, etc). Root cause: cross-site-node-mesh-limitation, macintel01 cannot reach CoreDNS service VIP. Fix: reschedule cloudflared off macintel01 (already cordoned) onto CP-LAN node; add nodeAffinity to keep it off cross-site nodes permanently.

title: CP apiservers down: OOM controller SIGKILLs apiserver + rpi01 stale etcd member
description: dell01/tp4 :6443 refused. Root cause: all 3 CP nodes (8GB) under sustained memory PSI pressure -> Talos userspace OOMController repeatedly SIGKILLs burstable pods incl kube-apiserver -> CrashLoopBackOff (2m40s) -> 6443 refused. Aggravated by rpi01 being a REMOVED etcd member (31210b111009dcd5) whose etcd restart-loops forever flooding leader tp4 with rejected-stream traffic. Death spiral: apiserver down -> all pods crashloop -> more mem churn -> more OOM.

title: ArgoCD cannot sync ANY app: provider-gcp-storage down -> conversion webhook breaks cluster cache
description: All ArgoCD Applications show sync=Unknown (cluster-wide). Root cause: crossplane provider-gcp-storage is fully down (both replicas Error/CrashLoopBackOff ~300 restarts), so its conversion webhook service (provider-gcp-storage.crossplane-system.svc:9443) has no healthy endpoint. ArgoCD's cluster cache build fails listing BucketIAMMember.storage.gcp.upbound.io: 'conversion webhook ... connect: connection refused'. With the cache broken, ArgoCD can compute sync status for NO app and reconciles nothing. provider-gcp-cloudplatform and provider-gcp-iam each have 1 Running + 1 Error replica; provider-gcp-storage has ZERO healthy. Pre-existing; blocks all GitOps.
design: Options: (a) fix provider-gcp-storage so its webhook endpoint comes up (check why it crashloops — likely GCP creds/ESO secret or resource limits); (b) if the GCP storage provider is unused/deprecated, remove the provider + its CRDs/conversion webhooks so they stop poisoning the cache; (c) temporarily delete the failing CustomResourceDefinition conversion webhook config. Verify after: kubectl -n argocd get applications shows Synced/OutOfSync (not Unknown).
acceptance_criteria: kubectl -n argocd get applications shows real sync status (not Unknown) for all apps; cilium app syncs and DS updateStrategy returns to RollingUpdate with KUBERNETES_SERVICE_HOST env on all nodes.
notes: RESOLVED 2026-06-29. Root cause: provider-gcp-storage crashlooped because its SafeStart RBAC precheck + CRD-gate watch went via the kube-proxy Service VIP 10.96.0.1, which LB's across control-plane apiservers on both LANs of this stretched cluster; the pod's node could only reach the same-LAN apiserver so ~2/3 of VIP connections timed out (same VIP pathology as the cilium issue). With the provider down its conversion webhook (storage.gcp.upbound.io) had no endpoint, breaking ArgoCD's cluster-wide resource listing -> all 50 apps stuck sync=Unknown. NOTE: patching CRD conversion strategy Webhook->None does NOT work — crossplane reconciles it back within seconds. FIX: dedicated DeploymentRuntimeConfig gcp-storage-pinned that pins the provider to dell01 (control-plane) and sets KUBERNETES_SERVICE_HOST=192.168.68.100/6443 so it talks to the LOCAL apiserver (no VIP, no cross-node hop). Provider now 1/1 stable on dell01, webhook endpoint up, conversion works, ArgoCD recovered (35 Synced/15 OutOfSync, 0 Unknown). Persisted in git: k8s/charts/crossplane-providers (commit a1022af4) — chart renders identical to live so ArgoCD sync is a no-op.
close_reason: provider-gcp-storage pinned to dell01 local apiserver (commit a1022af4); webhook back up, ArgoCD cache rebuilt, all apps have real sync status (no more Unknown).

Reproduce the cross-node failure, diff macarm01 vs the broken two, find why the macarm01 pattern doesn't hold for them.

title: VM monitoring stack dark: vmsingle empty, metrics not ingesting cluster-wide
description: FINDING (2026-07-05, needs proper investigation - do NOT mask with node pinning): VictoriaMetrics has NO data. vmsingle-vmks:8428 returns empty for EVERYTHING including its own vm_app_version and count(up) -> the store is empty (vmsingle pod restarted ~12h ago) and is NOT being refilled. Contributing factor: the whole VM stack (vmagent/vmalert/vmalertmanager) was scheduled on pc01 (broken VXLAN TX-offload datapath, memory pc01-crossnode-tcp-ROOTCAUSE) so vmagent could not remote-write. BUT: after vmagent rescheduled to a HEALTHY node (tp4), vmsingle STILL did not repopulate after ~2min -> so pc01 placement is not the whole story; vmagent->vmsingle remote-write is failing for another reason (check vmagent logs for remoteWrite errors, vmsingle health/storage, whether vmsingle also needs to be off a bad node). CONSEQUENCE: monitoring is blind cluster-wide; NO alert can fire, including the new ArgoCDClusterCacheDown VMRule (postmortem home-systems-57s). The scrape+rule are correctly deployed (controller exposes argocd_cluster_connection_status, VMServiceScrape target=up). A nodeAffinity band-aid to pin the stack off broken nodes was tried and REVERTED (c1e6cf8d) at user request because it masks rather than fixes. NEXT: debug vmagent remoteWrite -> vmsingle end to end; likely tied to the same node-datapath work being handled separately.

title: Root issue: cross-site guest nodes (macintel01/rpi01) keep hosting cluster-reachable workloads they cant serve
description: Recurring architectural fault line. macintel01+rpi01 are cross-site nodes with broken cross-node pod networking (Cilium VXLAN return path dies - see memory cross-site-node-mesh-limitation). Anything cluster-reachable placed on them (etcd peer, Longhorn replica/RWX share-manager, CoreDNS) becomes a landmine. Every recent incident is this same fault in a different subsystem: 2026-07-03/04 etcd-over-Tailscale quorum loss; 2026-07-05 argocd 502 pc01 datapath; 2026-07-05 hermes RWX share-manager placed on macintel01 unreachable from tp1. Each hand-fixed, none addressed root. Currently RE-ARMED: all nodes uncordoned + Longhorn allowScheduling=true on guests. Durable fix (encode declaratively in git/ArgoCD, NOT imperative kubectl): treat guests as compute-only - no Longhorn storage (per-node allowScheduling=false), mesh-critical infra pinned to reliable core (dell01/tp1/tp4/pc01/macarm01) via affinity (same pattern as CNPG nodeAffinity NotIn). Alt bigger fix: switch guests to Tailscale-IP-as-nodeIP (macarm01 pattern; caution - broke Service-VIP DNS before per macintel01-roaming-dhcp). Separately: dell01 sole-CP SPOF; if re-expanding to 3 CPs, peer etcd over LAN 192.168.68.x NOT Tailscale. FULL CONTEXT: docs/handoff-cross-site-node-fault-line.md

title: Postmortem: ArgoCD cluster sync blocked by crossplane conversion webhook (2026-07-05)
description: Follow-ups from .agents/postmortems/2026-07-05-argocd-crossplane-webhook-blocks-sync/PM.md. A single unreachable crossplane conversion webhook (provider on wedged pc01) aborted ArgoCD shared cluster cache -> all GitOps sync down. Durable fix: resource.exclusions for *.upbound.io + metallb.io (committed ea69d394 + follow-up). Adds ArgoCD VMServiceScrape + VMRule ArgoCDClusterCacheDown (argocd metrics were entirely absent before).

title: nic-offload-fix DaemonSet: disable virtio VXLAN tx-checksum offload on pc01
description: pc01 (Proxmox virtio VM, ens18) corrupts VXLAN-encapsulated TCP/UDP because guest TX checksum offload is on -> cross-node pod traffic into pc01 silently dropped (ICMP survives, node looks Ready). Root cause proven 2026-07-05: 'ethtool -K ens18 tx-checksum-ip-generic off' flips cross-node TCP 0/3->3/3. Not persistent (reverts on reboot). Proxmox/crossplane VM config exposes no offload toggle; Talos has no ethtool knob -> fix is a privileged DaemonSet in support-cluster chart (k8s/charts/support-cluster/templates/nic-offload-fix.yaml) scoped via nodeSelector to pc01, re-applying in a loop. Applied live fix already; DaemonSet added, pending ArgoCD sync + uncordon pc01.
close_reason: nic-offload-fix DaemonSet committed (500add7c), pushed, ArgoCD-synced and Running on pc01; tx-checksum-ip-generic=off verified, re-applied every 60s. pc01 uncordoned.

title: Split hermes STS: agent-only pod + hermes-workers deployment sharing RWX state
description: hermes-0 startup was coupled to repos-clone init, repos-sync/pages/dashboard sidecars and two RWO VCT volumes; any volume/attach hiccup blocked the agent for hours. Split: STS keeps only hermes-agent with state on a shared RWX PVC (hermes-state); new hermes-workers Deployment carries repos-clone/repos-sync/pages/dashboard mounting hermes-state + the existing repos RWX PVC. Includes state data migration from state-hermes-0.
notes: Split DONE + pushed (commits ed0ca98, 25bde370): agent-only StatefulSet + hermes-workers Deployment sharing RWX hermes-state (/opt/data) + repos (/workdir). State fully migrated from state-hermes-0 to hermes-state RWX, no data loss (kanban.db, state.db, sessions, home all copied; old vol had I/O errors on top-level files but dirs intact after reattach). Agent STS applied directly to bypass wedged ArgoCD; reaches Init:0/3 but cannot finish because CSI mount / pod DNS (CoreDNS 10.96.0.10) intermittently time out during recurring control-plane flaps. Fixed along the way: power-cycled wedged tp1/tp4 (etcd 2380 conn leak ~2300 vs ~14 healthy); bounced crashlooping crossplane provider-gcp-storage, argocd-repo-server (OOM exit137), longhorn csi-attacher/provisioner; removed dead CoreDNS replica on hammered tp1; UNCORDONED dell01/tp1/tp4 (left cordoned from prior incident -> pc01 was only schedulable node & CPU-req 99% full -> nothing could place). BLOCKER = documented root cause (memory cluster-down-etcd-tailscale-quorum): etcd peers over Tailscale 100.x for co-located LAN CPs; TS wobble -> etcd write timeouts -> apiserver crashloop. PERMANENT FIX pending: move etcd peering to LAN 192.168.68.x.
close_reason: Split shipped and running: agent-only STS + hermes-workers Deployment sharing RWX hermes-state+repos, both Running on tp1, dell01 clean, endpoints live.

title: macarm01 node unstable - resource-starved UTM VM flapping NotReady
description: macarm01 (Apple Silicon UTM VM, TS-IP nodeIP 100.91.131.67) flaps NotReady: kubelet stops posting status, talos apid + kubelet ports open but TLS/gRPC handshakes time out ~2/3 (CPU starvation). tailscale ping pongs fine. Underlying cause of tailscale ingress outage. Needs host-side fix on the Mac (free CPU/RAM, hard-restart VM, or demote/remove from cluster).

title: Cluster-wide kube-apiserver crashloop (dell01+tp1 SIGKILL 137/liveness, ~90min); etcd healthy
description: After a Tailscale/network turbulence window (~2026-07-02 13:2x), kube-apiserver on dell01 (18 restarts) and tp1 (16) are in CrashLoopBackOff, SIGKILL exit 137 via failed liveness probe. tp4 + rpi01 apiservers currently stable (0 restarts ~5min). etcd HEALTHY: 4 members (dell01,tp1,tp4,rpi01) stable leader tp4 raft term 1572 no churn no alarms. Nodes NOT OOM (dell01 1.8G avail, tp1 1.5G, tp4 0.7G avail). /livez + /readyz pass on up apiservers (all checks ok) => intermittent liveness timeouts, no fatal log. Noise in logs: crossplane provider-gcp-storage conversion webhook (svc 10.107.74.20:9443) down (provider pod 127 restarts) causing failed LIST on storage.gcp.upbound.io CRDs; heavy service-account-token-invalidated auth errors (churn symptom). BLOCKS: completing CP retopo (cannot safely demote tp1 while its + dell01 apiservers flap). Suspect network/etcd-over-Tailscale transient latency causing apiserver->etcd /livez timeouts, or webhook storm. Needs root-cause: check kubelet liveness probe timeout config, apiserver --etcd-servers latency, disable/repair provider-gcp-storage webhook, correlate restart timestamps with Tailscale relay events.

title: istio-cni cmdAdd times out on cross-site nodes (10.96.0.1) -> Longhorn crashloop -> pg can't scale -> litellm role uncreated
description: istio-cni-node's CNI plugin calls apiserver at Service VIP 10.96.0.1 during cmdAdd ambient check; VIP LBs across all CP backends incl. cross-site-unreachable ones -> i/o timeout -> pod sandbox fails on macintel01/rpi01 -> longhorn-manager/csi-plugin crashloop -> no CSI driver -> pg-2 PVC can't attach -> cluster NotReady -> CNPG defers litellm role/db creation. Fix: point istio-cni at Talos KubePrism (localhost:7445) + shareHostNetworkNamespace, same as cilium.
notes: istio-cni FIXED via KubePrism (committed 42560d8, pods 1/1 on both nodes). Deeper root cause remains: pod-netns traffic to apiserver Service VIP 10.96.0.1 (cilium-DNAT'd to CP LAN 192.168.68.x) is unreachable on macintel01/rpi01 -> longhorn-manager blocks on apiserver at init, never binds :9502 healthz, stays unready -> csi-plugin/node-driver-registrar cascade -> no CSI driver. hostNetwork pods work (host has Tailscale route to CP LAN); pod-netns pods don't (cilium datapath doesn't egress via host route). Fix lives in cilium routing/masquerade for 192.168.68.0/24 OR node Tailscale route (caveat: --accept-routes broke pod CIDR/DNS before). Nodes currently unreachable via kubelet:10250 and talosd:50000.
close_reason: Hermes/litellm restored. Root chain fixed: (1) istio-cni pointed at KubePrism on cross-site nodes (was breaking pod sandbox creation); (2) tailscale --accept-routes on rpi01 (+dropped 10.96/12 advert) so it reaches apiserver; (3) pinned cnpg-postgres by digest (stale :16.10 lacked pgsodium_getkey -> replica PANIC); (4) pg replicas kept off off-LAN nodes via nodeAffinity + cordon; (5) litellm postgres role/db provisioned + 1Password [password] typo fixed. litellm pod 1/1, /health/liveliness OK, pg 3/3 healthy.

title: Retopo control plane: CP = dell01+tp1+tp4, demote rpi01+macintel01 to workers
description:
Decision (user): make the control plane the 3 well-connected home-LAN nodes (dell01, tp1, tp4) and demote the flaky cross-site nodes (rpi01, macintel01) to workers. Fixes both the apiserver-VIP unreachability (home-systems-ick/vn0) AND cross-site pod-network fragility (KCM leader on rpi01 cant reach longhorn webhook) in one move.

SAFE ORDER (never drop below etcd quorum): grow to 5 then shrink to 3.
Phase 0 (safe): author controlplane machineconfigs for tp1/tp4 (dell01 model: machine.type controlplane, cluster CA key op://, etcd.advertisedSubnets 100.64.0.0/10, apiserver certSANs LAN+TS, allowSchedulingOnControlPlanes, kubelet nodeIP.validSubnets 192.168.68.0/24, tailscale advertise 192.168.68.0/24 + NO accept-routes, kubePrism). Update nostos/config.yaml tp1/tp4 role=controlplane + tailscale_ip. Fix stale rpi01 config (ip 192.168.9.170, ts 100.127.199.21).
Phase 1 grow: cordon/drain + reset + reinstall tp1 as CP (Turing Pi BMC), verify 4 etcd members healthy + Ready; then tp4 -> 5 members quorum 3.
Phase 2 shrink: talosctl reset rpi01 (graceful etcd leave) -> reapply worker config -> rejoin worker; verify 4 members; then macintel01 -> 3 members (all home-LAN), quorum 2.
Phase 3 verify: kubernetes Service endpoints = 3 reachable home-LAN IPs; pg initdb (home-systems-ick) completes; uncordon.
RISKS: tp1/tp4 disk wipe + workload reschedule (tp4 hosts cnpg-operator); ARM64 CP configs never validated; etcd quorum-critical - one node at a time with verification; multi-hour, degraded during transitions.
notes: DONE 2026-07-01. Executed non-destructively (NO wipe, NO data loss): promoted tp1+tp4 worker->controlplane via in-place talosctl apply-config --mode=reboot (Talos accepts the machine.type change with a reboot; disks preserved incl all 12 Longhorn volumes on tp1). New CPs auto-joined etcd (grow to 5, no talosctl bootstrap -> no split-brain). Then demoted macintel01+rpi01 to workers via in-place apply-config --mode=reboot + talosctl etcd remove-member <id> (by hex ID, not hostname). Final: etcd=3 members dell01/tp1/tp4 (all 192.168.68.0/24, peering over Tailscale=direct LAN), HEALTH OK, quorum 2. kubernetes Service endpoints now only 3 LAN IPs -> apiserver-VIP timeouts eliminated. Removed stale control-plane label/taint from rpi01+macintel01, uncordoned macintel01; all nodes Ready. tp1/tp4 templates + macintel01/rpi01 templates + config.yaml roles updated in git (NOT committed).
close_reason: Control-plane retopo to dell01+tp1+tp4 (home-LAN) done in-place with no data loss; kubernetes Service now has only 3 reachable LAN apiserver endpoints, eliminating the 10.96.0.1 cross-site timeouts that crashlooped cnpg-operator + longhorn csi-provisioner. postgres pg cluster healthy (pg-1 primary). etcd 3 members healthy.

title: CNPG pg cluster stuck + cnpg-operator/longhorn csi-provisioner crashloop: apiserver Service VIP (10.96.0.1) balances to unreachable cross-site control-plane nodes
description: Root cause: kubernetes Service 10.96.0.1 has 3 apiserver endpoints on 3 different LANs (dell01 192.168.68.100 home, rpi01 192.168.9.170 remote, macintel01 192.168.0.91 remote roaming). Worker pods (tp1/tp4) load-balance across all 3; ~2/3 of connections hit a cross-site apiserver they cannot route to -> context deadline exceeded. Leader-election controllers (cnpg-operator 1467 restarts, longhorn csi-provisioner 3 replicas ~1800 restarts) lose their lease on renewal and crashloop. Smoking gun: csi-provisioner 'Failed to update lock ... Put https://10.96.0.1:443/.../leases/driver-longhorn-io: context deadline exceeded'. Downstream: pg CNPG cluster stuck 'Setting up primary' 0 pods; pg-1-initdb job terminally Failed (BackoffLimitExceeded, 7 fails) and operator won't retry a terminal job. Fix A (decision needed): demote flaky cross-site CPs to workers vs. move apiserver advertise addresses to Tailscale IPs. Fix B: after A stable, recreate pg Cluster (argocd app 'postgres') to rerun initdb.
close_reason: Root cause (apiserver Service VIP 10.96.0.1 load-balancing to unreachable cross-site control-plane nodes) eliminated by control-plane retopo f38: CP is now dell01+tp1+tp4 on the home LAN, endpoints all reachable. cnpg-operator + longhorn csi-provisioner no longer crashloop; pg cluster healthy.

title: macintel01 etcd stuck out of raft consensus after reboot; kube-apiserver-macintel01 crashlooping
description: During the cilium fix I rebooted macintel01 (to clear a stale .26 apiserver endpoint after it roamed to .91). After reboot, macintel01's etcd came back as a voting member but is STUCK: raft index frozen at ~28464372 (term 1051) across repeated samples while the dell01 leader advances normally (28490157->28490480, term 1054). etcd health flaps 'context deadline exceeded'. Because local etcd is wedged, kube-apiserver-macintel01 is in CrashLoopBackOff (8 restarts, 5m backoff) and macintel01 is NOT in the kubernetes Service endpoints. Time is synced (not a clock issue); cause is the unreliable cross-site Tailscale etcd link + the roaming-CP being a poor etcd voter (documented in macintel01-roaming-dhcp + rpi01 memories).
design: Cluster is STABLE on 2/3 quorum (dell01 leader + rpi01 healthy) but with ZERO margin — macintel01 is a dead voter. Recovery options (need human decision, control-plane SPOF-adjacent): (a) restart etcd service on macintel01 and watch raft index catch up; (b) etcd member remove 3dcef377d92076af + wipe /var/lib/etcd + rejoin; (c) reconsider making macintel01 a worker not a control-plane (laptop/roaming VM is a poor always-on etcd voter).
acceptance_criteria: macintel01 etcd raft index advancing in lockstep with leader; kube-apiserver-macintel01 1/1 Running; macintel01 back in 'kubectl get endpoints kubernetes' as 192.168.0.91.
notes: RESOLVED 2026-06-29. Root blocker after the wipe/rejoin was that macintel01 stayed a LEARNER and Talos never auto-promotes a learner whose linearizable health check times out (chicken-and-egg: learner cant pass the check, so Talos wont promote; talosctl has NO promote subcommand). Since no local etcdctl/tailnet access and etcd only listens on the TS IP, ran it as a one-off privileged hostNetwork pod on dell01 (image registry.k8s.io/etcd:v3.6.11, imagePullPolicy IfNotPresent), etcd admin certs read from /system/secrets/etcd via talosctl and injected as a Secret (hostPath to /system/secrets is NOT visible to the kubelet namespace — use a Secret). Result: member promoted -> etcd health check successful -> Talos rendered kube-apiserver static pod -> kube-apiserver-macintel01 1/1 Running -> macintel01 back in 'kubectl get endpoints kubernetes' as 192.168.0.91. 3 healthy voters again (dell01+rpi01+macintel01), quorum margin restored. Recovery pod + secret deleted. NOTE: TS IP changed to 100.118.143.56 (EPHEMERAL wipe reset tailscaled) — verify certSANs in nostos/templates/macintel01.yaml. Underlying architecture caveat still stands: roaming laptop VM is a marginal cross-site etcd voter.

title: dell01: remove failing WALRAM /dev/sda from config to restore control plane
description: WALRAM-256GB SATA SSD (/dev/sda, Longhorn storage) failed after power-cut: READ FPDMA QUEUED timeouts + XFS corruption. Talos block.MountController loops retrying the bad mount, stalling volume phase -> kubelet/cri/etcd never register -> dell01 NotReady. NVMe OS disk healthy. Provisional fix: drop /dev/sda volume from nostos/templates/dell01.yaml, render+apply. Replace SSD later.
notes: RESOLVED 2026-06-28. dell01 control-plane restored. What actually happened: removing /dev/sda from nostos/templates/dell01.yaml + render was correct, but apply could NOT persist while the node was wedged: (1) auto/reboot mode hung at reboot-sequence phase 7 (can't unmount the stuck sda mount); (2) --mode=staged with talosctl 1.13.4 against the 1.13.3 server silently failed to register (version skew) - boot kept loading old STATE config with sda; (3) --mode=no-reboot rejected (disk change needs reboot). FIX: physically removed the dead WALRAM SSD, then with the disk gone the reboot's unmount phase no longer hung, so 'talosctl-1.13.3 apply-config --mode=auto' persisted the clean config to STATE -> clean boot, cri/kubelet/etcd up, rejoined etcd. ALSO defragged etcd dell01+macintel01 (449MB->~50MB each). LESSON: match talosctl client to server version for staged applies; a configured-but-missing user disk still wedges boot (lstat error loop) until config is fixed.
close_reason: dell01 restored to cluster (Ready); dead WALRAM SSD removed from config + physically pulled; clean config persisted via version-matched talosctl auto-apply; etcd defragged

title: Rotate expired GHCR PAT in 1Password (blocks agents + syscd-app image pulls)
description: The ghcr.io pull credential stored in 1Password (synced to the ghcr-image-pull-secret ExternalSecret) is EXPIRED/REVOKED. Evidence: the dockerconfigjson decodes to user=yurifrl, password=ghp_... (classic PAT, 40 chars). GitHub https://api.github.com/user returns HTTP 401 with that token (a valid PAT always authenticates /user regardless of scopes -> 401 means dead token). Consequently ghcr token request https://ghcr.io/token?scope=repository:yurifrl/agents:pull returns {"code":"DENIED"} and the manifest pull returns 403. This blocks BOTH ImagePullBackOff apps: agents (ghcr.io/yurifrl/agents:dev) and syscd-app (ghcr.io/yurifrl/syscd-app:latest). FIX (user-only): generate a new GitHub PAT with read:packages scope, update the 1Password item that feeds ghcr-image-pull-secret, let ESO resync, then delete the failing pods so they re-pull. Agent cannot fabricate/rotate credentials.

EVIDENCE:
- 'cilium-health status' from tp1: 6/7 reachable; rpi01 (192.168.0.170) is 0/1 (node + health endpoint UNREACHABLE from in-cluster).
- kubernetes Service 10.96.0.1:443 has 3 backends: 192.168.68.100 (dell01), 192.168.68.91 (macintel01), 192.168.0.170:6443 (rpi01). Cilium svc id 38.

FIX (CP machineconfig change, do when on-site/attended):
- Edit nostos/templates/rpi01.yaml lines 22-24: change kubelet nodeIP.validSubnets from '192.168.0.0/24' to '100.64.0.0/10' (Tailscale CGNAT), matching the other remote nodes. etcd already uses advertisedSubnets 100.64.0.0/10 so etcd peering is unaffected.
- Then: nostos render rpi01 && nostos apply rpi01 . rpi01 kubelet restarts and re-registers InternalIP as its 100.x Tailscale IP; the kubernetes endpoint becomes routable; timeouts stop.
- CAUTION: rpi01 is the flaky offsite control-plane (quorum-fragile per bd memories). This changes a CP node's IP -> verify etcd stays healthy (etcdctl member list) and 'nostos status' Ready before/after. Observe before destroy.

ALT (if rpi01 should not be a CP at all): drain+remove rpi01 from control plane, but that drops etcd to 2 members = quorum trap. Prefer the nodeIP fix.

NOT done unattended per AGENTS.md AI-agent provisioning guardrails.

title: Finish rpi01 etcd join (stale learner peer URL on LAN, not CGNAT)
description:
rpi01 joined dell01 etcd as a LEARNER but registered peer URL https://192.168.0.170:2380 (LAN). dell01 (192.168.68.x) cannot reach that, so rpi01 never promotes. dell01 already advertises CGNAT (100.82.148.37); DO NOT apply dell01 (needless etcd restart on sole controlplane).

STATE (2026-06-15):
- rpi01 OFFLINE on tailnet (last seen 9:45 PM GMT-3). Wait until 100.79.232.96 is online.
- rpi01 template fixed + applied: --accept-routes removed, TS_EXTRA_ARGS=--reset added (cleared crashloop), kubelet nodeIP.validSubnets [192.168.0.0/24], etcd.advertisedSubnets [100.64.0.0/10]. No 10.244.0.0/16 route hijack (verified).
- All template/config + nostos submodule edits are UNCOMMITTED.

RESUME (need both nodes visible — Tailscale up on the Mac):
1. Confirm rpi01 online: tailscale status | grep rpi01  (and talosctl -n 192.168.0.170 --endpoints 192.168.0.170 version)
2. Remove stale learner from dell01 (SAFE, learner=no quorum impact):
   talosctl --talosconfig ~/.talos/config -n 192.168.68.100 --endpoints 192.168.68.100 etcd remove-member 018a72e0dc96b74f
3. Wipe rpi01 etcd so it rejoins fresh with CGNAT peer URL:
   talosctl --talosconfig ~/.talos/config -n 192.168.0.170 --endpoints 192.168.0.170 reset --graceful=false --reboot --system-labels-to-wipe EPHEMERAL   (verify exact flag for etcd-only wipe first)
4. Verify rpi01 rejoins with CGNAT peer URL + promotes:
   talosctl ... -n 192.168.68.100 etcd members   (expect rpi01 peer https://100.79.232.96:2380, LEARNER false)
   kubectl get nodes
notes: RESOLVED the etcd join. Root causes were NOT network: (1) kexec is broken on Pi 4 -> warm reboot leaves no-RTC clock at epoch -> apid cert minted at 1970 -> API lockout (fix: kexec_load_disabled=1 kernel arg + reboot --mode powercycle; confirmed talos#6046 + r/TalosLinux + maintainer xrothgarx). (2) public NTP-via-DNS fails at boot -> use IP-literal NTP servers (162.159.200.123 cloudflare, 216.239.35.0 google). (3) etcd DB was 922MB (60MB in use) -> snapshot to learner never finished over tailnet -> defragged dell01 to 61MB. (4) etcd peer must advertise CGNAT via etcd.advertisedSubnets [100.64.0.0/10], NEVER --accept-routes (breaks pod CIDR). KEY: apid auto-rotates cert on NTP sync, so a cold boot self-heals the lockout -- no reflash needed. OUTCOME: rpi01 joined + promoted to voter, both etcd members HEALTHY. Remaining: rpi01 k8s NotReady (cilium agent 0/1, cross-site CNI issue - separate).
close_reason: etcd join complete: rpi01 promoted to voter, both members healthy. Cilium NotReady tracked separately.

title: nostos cluster bootstrap controller
description: Replace scattered Talos bootstrap YAML + manual Cilium install with a self-healing in-cluster controller (nostos-bootstrap). Single source (config.yaml), config-driven ArgoCD app generation pointing at user repo, ordered bootstrap (Cilium -> ESO -> ArgoCD -> apps), single-pane logs/status.
design:
# Design: nostos in-cluster bootstrap controller (`nostos-bootstrap`)

Status: draft
Date: 2026-06-06
Owner: Yuri
Related: `.agents/drafts/nostos-pxe-reliable-ai-friendly.md`, nostos submodule

---

## 1. Problem

Today a fresh `talos-default` cluster comes up via a pile of **loose YAML
hand-stuffed into the Talos machineconfig** (`nostos/templates/dell01.yaml`):

- `extraManifests:` → 2 GitHub raw URLs (argocd, app-of-apps)
- `inlineManifests:` → 5 blobs (3 namespaces, the 1Password Connect secret, the
  ESO `ClusterSecretStore`)
- and the CNI (Cilium) isn't wired at all — it's installed **manually** by hand
  (`kubectl apply` of a rendered manifest).

Consequences:
- **No single source of truth** — bootstrap state is smeared across the
  machineconfig, GitHub raw URLs, and a human running kubectl.
- **Not self-healing** — Talos applies inlineManifests at cluster-init only;
  nothing reconciles drift or recovers a half-bootstrap.
- **No observability** — there's no one place to see "is the cluster bootstrap
  healthy and why."
- **Fragile ordering** — CNI must exist before anything else can schedule, but
  nothing enforces that.

## 2. Goals / non-goals

**Goals**
- One declarative source (`config.yaml`) for everything required to get a
  cluster from *bare apiserver* to *ArgoCD reconciling the user's repo*.
- A **self-healing** in-cluster controller that reconciles the bootstrap tier
  continuously (not a run-once Job) and **logs very well** — a **single place to
  look** for bootstrap health.
- **Config-driven app generation**: the user sets their GitOps repo (+ paths) in
  config; the controller **generates the ArgoCD Application(s)** that point at
  that repo. The user never hand-writes the root app.
- **Deterministic ordering**: CNI → secrets/ESO → ArgoCD → generated apps.
- Reduce the machineconfig to the **one irreducible secret** + the controller.

**Non-goals**
- Replacing ArgoCD. The controller hands off to ArgoCD and never manages the
  user's individual apps.
- Managing day-2 app config (that stays in the user's git repo, owned by ArgoCD).
- Being a general operator framework — scope is strictly cluster bootstrap.

## 3. Architecture

```
config.yaml  ──nostos render──►  machineconfig:
  (bootstrap:)                     ├─ inlineManifest: op-credentials Secret  (root secret, irreducible)
  cilium: {...}                    ├─ inlineManifest: bootstrap-config ConfigMap (rendered from config.yaml)
  argocd:  {version, ...}          └─ inlineManifest: nostos-bootstrap Deployment + SA/RBAC
  repos:   [{url, path}]                         │
  namespaces: [...]                              ▼
                                   nostos-bootstrap controller (self-healing, hostNetwork)
                                     reconcile loop, ordered:
                                       1. Cilium (CNI)          ──► nodes Ready
                                       2. ESO + ClusterSecretStore (uses root secret)
                                       3. ArgoCD
                                       4. generate ArgoCD Application(s) from config → point at user repo
                                     then keep tiers 1-4 healthy; expose status + logs
                                               │
                                               ▼
                                   ArgoCD ──► syncs everything else from the user's repo
```

### 3.1 Three tiers (the cleanup)

| Tier | Lives in | Contents |
|---|---|---|
| **Config (source of truth)** | `config.yaml` `bootstrap:` | Cilium values, ArgoCD version, user repo URL(s)+path(s), root namespaces |
| **nostos-owned (irreducible, machineconfig)** | rendered inline | the one root 1Password Connect Secret + bootstrap ConfigMap + the controller Deployment/SA/RBAC |
| **git / ArgoCD** | user's repo | all actual apps; ArgoCD owns them |

Today's 5 inline blobs + 2 extra URLs + manual Cilium **collapse into**:
`config.yaml` → 1 root secret + 1 ConfigMap + 1 controller.

## 4. Components

### 4.1 The controller binary (`cmd/bootstrap` in the nostos repo)
- A **self-healing reconcile loop** (Deployment, single replica, leader-elect
  ready for future HA). Runs `hostNetwork: true`, tolerates the
  `node.kubernetes.io/not-ready` taint, pins to control-plane — so it can come
  up **before the CNI exists** (same trick Cilium's own DaemonSet uses).
- Reads desired state from the **bootstrap-config ConfigMap** (rendered from
  `config.yaml`) + the root Secret.
- Applies bootstrap tiers via **server-side-apply (client-go)** against embedded,
  parameterized manifests. **No Helm at runtime** (keeps the image small — see
  issue #2).
- Reconciles on an interval + on watch; **idempotent**; recreates anything
  missing (self-heal).
- **Logging**: structured (JSON + human), one line per reconcile decision, plus a
  rolled-up **status** written to a `ConfigMap`/CR (`BootstrapStatus`) so "a
  single place to look" = `kubectl logs deploy/nostos-bootstrap` and/or
  `kubectl get bootstrapstatus`.

### 4.2 Image + CI
- New tiny **multi-arch** image (amd64 + arm64) → `ghcr.io/yurifrl/nostos-bootstrap`.
- Static Go binary, distroless/scratch base.
- CI builds + pushes on tag; tag is pinned in `config.yaml`/machineconfig.

### 4.3 nostos render changes
- New `bootstrap:` block in `config.yaml`.
- `nostos render` emits exactly three inline manifests: root Secret (op:// resolved),
  bootstrap ConfigMap (serialized `bootstrap:` block), controller Deployment+RBAC
  (image tag from config).
- Remove the 5 hand-written inline blobs + 2 extraManifest URLs from templates.

### 4.4 App generation
- `config.yaml` lists the user's repo(s): `repos: [{url, path, revision}]`.
- The controller **generates one ArgoCD root Application per repo entry**
  (app-of-apps style) pointing at the user's repo/path. The user manages the
  actual app manifests **in their repo**; the controller only creates the root
  app(s). Single config knob → apps appear.

## 5. Bootstrap ordering (the reconcile sequence)

1. **Cilium** — apply; wait until all nodes report `Ready` (CNI up).
2. **ESO + `ClusterSecretStore`** — apply CRDs + operator; create the store using
   the root Secret; wait until store is `Valid`.
3. **ArgoCD** — apply; wait until the API/server is healthy.
4. **Generate apps** — render ArgoCD Application(s) from `config.repos`, apply.
5. **Hand off** — ArgoCD reconciles the rest. Controller keeps tiers 1-4 healthy
   and reports status; it never touches tier-5 apps.

Each step gates the next (wait + timeout + retry). Failures are logged with
cause and surfaced in `BootstrapStatus`; the loop retries (self-heal).

## 6. Self-healing model & boundary with ArgoCD

- Controller **exclusively owns** Cilium, ESO+store, ArgoCD, and the generated
  root Application(s). ArgoCD does **not** manage these → no split-brain.
- ArgoCD owns everything in the user's repo.
- "Self-heal" = if Cilium/ESO/ArgoCD/root-app drift or vanish, the controller
  re-applies from config. Upgrades happen by changing `config.yaml` → re-render →
  re-apply machineconfig → controller reconciles (see issue #5/#7 on the
  config-update loop).

## 7. Observability ("single place to look")

- `kubectl logs deploy/nostos-bootstrap -f` — ordered, structured reconcile log.
- `kubectl get bootstrapstatus -o yaml` (or a status ConfigMap) — per-tier state
  (`Cilium: Ready`, `ESO: Valid`, `ArgoCD: Healthy`, `Apps: Synced`), last error,
  last reconcile time.
- Optional: Prometheus metrics endpoint for the VM/Grafana stack.

## 8. Potential issues / risks

1. **CNI/scheduling paradox** — the controller pod can't get a CNI IP because it
   *is* installing the CNI. Must run `hostNetwork: true` + not-ready toleration +
   control-plane node-selector. Verified-possible (Cilium DS does it); must be
   built exactly so or it never schedules.
2. **"Small" vs. capability** — installing Cilium/ArgoCD well wants Helm; Helm is
   heavy. Decision baked in: **embed rendered manifests + client-go SSA, no Helm
   at runtime.** client-go still adds ~tens of MB; "super small" is relative
   (single static binary, ~30-50MB image), not tiny.
3. **Root-secret irreducibility** — the 1Password Connect secret must still be
   nostos-injected into the machineconfig; can't reach zero loose secret YAML,
   only **one**.
4. **Multi-arch + registry reachability pre-CNI** — image must be multi-arch
   (mixed fleet). Pulled over host network before CNI; public ghcr is fine, a
   **private** image needs pull creds = another root secret. Offsite rpi01 pulls
   over Tailscale at boot — confirm the tailnet route is up before CNI.
5. **Config-update loop is heavier than git** — because bootstrap config rides in
   the machineconfig, changing it = `nostos render` + re-apply machineconfig +
   controller reconcile. Fine for rare bootstrap-tier changes; **do not** put
   fast-moving app config here (that's why apps stay in the user's git repo).
6. **Controller vs ArgoCD overlap** — must keep a hard boundary (controller owns
   tiers 1-4, ArgoCD owns tier 5). If the user's repo also defines Cilium/ArgoCD,
   they'll fight. Document/guard against it.
7. **Version coupling** — image tag pinned in machineconfig; bumping the
   controller = re-render + re-apply every control-plane. CI must build/push/tag
   the image in lockstep with nostos releases.
8. **Single replica SPOF on bootstrap** — on a single-control-plane cluster the
   controller runs on dell01; if dell01 is down, no self-heal. Acceptable at
   bootstrap; leader-election hook lets it scale when rpi01 joins.
9. **Powerful SA** — controller needs near-cluster-admin (CNI, CRDs, RBAC).
   Scope tightly; consider a narrower role once bootstrapped.
10. **Talos applies inline at init only** — the controller Deployment survives as
    a normal workload after init, but if it's ever fully deleted, only a
    machineconfig re-apply (or talosctl upgrade-k8s) recreates it. Document the
    re-trigger.
11. **"Is the controller worth it?"** — Talos extraManifests already fetch
    argocd+app-of-apps by URL. The controller earns its keep via **ordering +
    wait-for-Ready + self-heal + config-driven app-gen + single-pane status**.
    If those weren't required, "Cilium as an extraManifest URL + ArgoCD does the
    rest" would be far less to build. We are building it because self-heal +
    single source + observability are the explicit goals.

## 9. Open decisions

- **Status surface**: lightweight (a status `ConfigMap`) vs. a real
  `BootstrapStatus` CRD (+ CRD lifecycle). CRD is nicer UX, more to maintain.
- **App-gen granularity**: one root app-of-apps per repo entry (simplest) vs.
  generating individual `Application`s from a config list.
- **Cilium upgrade UX**: via `config.yaml` + machineconfig re-apply (consistent,
  heavier) vs. letting ArgoCD adopt Cilium for day-2 (splits ownership — not
  recommended given the self-heal boundary).
- **Reconcile interval / watch**: poll interval value; whether to watch ArgoCD
  health to re-drive ordering.

## 10. Dependency / sequencing (critical)

The nostos-render injection is the **terminal** step, not the first one, because
the YAML nostos injects into the Talos machineconfig **references the controller
image**. So the chain is strictly:

1. **nostos repo published on GitHub** — nostos is currently a local submodule
   (`.submodules/nostos/`). It must exist as a real GitHub repo so it can host
   the `cmd/bootstrap` source **and** the CI that builds + pushes the multi-arch
   image to `ghcr.io/yurifrl/nostos-bootstrap`. **No repo → no image → nothing to
   inject.**
2. **Image built + pushed** (multi-arch, tagged) — the tag the injected YAML
   pins must actually exist in ghcr before any cluster boots with it.
3. **Only then** can `nostos render` inject the controller Deployment (+ config
   ConfigMap + root Secret) into Talos, because the Deployment's `image:` must
   resolve at pull time.

## 11. Migration plan (today → this)

1. **Publish the nostos repo to GitHub** (prerequisite for everything — see §10).
2. Build `cmd/bootstrap` + image + CI (multi-arch ghcr). Validate on a throwaway
   Talos VM cluster end-to-end (bare → ArgoCD reconciling).
3. Add `bootstrap:` schema to `config.yaml`; teach `nostos render` to emit the
   3 inline manifests; delete the 5 hand-written blobs + 2 extra URLs.
4. Cut over on a rebuild: `nostos up dell01` → controller bootstraps Cilium →
   ESO → ArgoCD → generated apps; verify single-pane status green.
5. Retire the manual `nostos/manifests/cilium/*` stopgap (keep one rendered
   break-glass copy if desired).
6. Wire rpi01 the same way (offsite); verify multi-arch + Tailscale-pre-CNI pull.

---

### Decisions captured from this discussion
- Controller is **self-healing + logs very well + single place to look** (not a
  one-shot Job).
- Apps are **generated from config**, pointing at the **user's repo** (user
  configures the repo; we create the root app).
- Controller **owns bootstrap ordering**; goal is guaranteed bootstrap + one
  place to look.

title: P3: NDJSON per-MAC event stream + nostos pxe status --output json
description:
Issue 4 (flying blind). The AI ran the install in cmux splits whose output it couldn't read; it depended on the user pasting logs and phone photos of the console. There was no per-node state anywhere.

Current state: progress is an in-memory channel (HTTPRequests -> tap -> provisioner.Event). No persisted per-MAC state, no queryable status, no machine-readable event stream.

Fix: emit an NDJSON event stream per MAC covering the boot lifecycle: discover -> tftp -> kernel -> initramfs -> config -> maintenance -> apid -> bootstrap. Add 'nostos pxe status --output json' that reports per-node state. Honor the nostos output contract (list commands emit NDJSON, object commands emit one JSON object; structured errors on stdout + one-line hint on stderr). Document a --log-json detached pattern so a long serve can be tailed from a readable logfile.

Depends on the source-IP tap fix so events are attributed to the right node.
acceptance_criteria: serve maintains per-MAC lifecycle state spanning discover/tftp/kernel/initramfs/config/maintenance/apid/bootstrap; 'nostos pxe status --output json' returns NDJSON per node and a single object for a specific node per the output contract; events are emitted as NDJSON suitable for a detached --log-json tail; an AI can determine node progress without reading a TTY or photos.
close_reason: Added per-MAC PXE event observability. New internal/pxe/events.go: Phase constants (discover..ready) with ordinal ordering; Event{ts,mac,ip,phase,message}; EventStore appends NDJSON to <State>/pxe/events.ndjson (mutex, dir-create, missing-file tolerant, nil-safe); pure ClassifyHTTPPath (boot.ipxe->tftp, vmlinuz->kernel, initramfs->initramfs, /configs/<mac>.yaml->config+MAC); pure ParseDnsmasqLine (DISCOVER/REQUEST->discover, DHCPACK->tftp w/ IP+MAC binding, sent ipxe.efi->tftp); pure FoldState builds IP->MAC map from DHCPACK then relabels IP-only HTTP events onto the MAC, folding to NodeState at furthest phase. serve.go records at source in loggingMiddleware (NO two-consumer channel bug) + tees dnsmasq stdout/stderr via io.MultiWriter+line recorder; --log-json mirrors events to a file. 'nostos pxe status [NODE]' joins config by MACHyphen, list=NDJSON+table, single=object, --fields; unknown MACs surface known:no (seeds .8). schema pxe.status. events_test.go hermetic. Full suite 262 pass.

title: P3: config-fetch tap must match node source IP/MAC, not any HTTP hit
description:
Issue 5 (false signals, tool side). The AI's own curl to the config URL tripped nostos's 'config-fetched' detector, producing a false 'installing' reported as progress.

Current state: internal/pxe/serve.go loggingMiddleware relays only r.URL.Path to the HTTPRequests channel (no source IP/MAC). internal/provisioner/pxe/pxe.go Boot tap matches path == '/configs/<mac-hyphen>.yaml' and emits config-fetched for ANY requester.

Fix: the config-fetch tap must match the node's source IP/MAC, not any HTTP hit. Plumb the request's RemoteAddr (and, where derivable, the node MAC) through the HTTPRequests channel; the tap should only fire config-fetched when the source matches the target node. A curl from the operator host must NOT trip it.
acceptance_criteria: loggingMiddleware forwards request source IP alongside the path; the pxe provisioner tap only emits config-fetched when the source IP matches the target node (not the operator host or any other client); a curl to the config URL from the operator host does not produce a config-fetched event; unit test covers source-IP filtering.
close_reason: serve.go: httpRequests now chan HTTPRequest{Path,SourceIP}; loggingMiddleware stamps source IP from RemoteAddr; added Server.LocalIPs() (loopback + bound interface IPs). provisioner/pxe Boot tap snapshots LocalIPs and tracks boot-chain source IPs; config-fetched is gated by pure helper shouldEmitConfigFetched(req, localIPs, bootChainIPs) = not-empty AND not-local/loopback AND previously fetched boot chain. An operator-host curl no longer trips it. Tests: TestLocalIPs*, TestLoggingMiddlewareStampsSourceIP*, TestShouldEmitConfigFetched (operator/node/unknown cases). Full suite 232 pass.

title: P2: PXE serve as long-running daemon + fail-fast sudo_required + no serve-timeout
description:
Issues 2 & 3 (sudo relaunch loop + serve lifecycle). The ~10-min serve timeout killed the server repeatedly; the install needs the node restarted WHILE the server serves, so timeouts forced a blind relaunch-and-resync dance. Each relaunch re-prompted for sudo.

Current state:
- internal/cluster/orchestrate.go InstallOpts.ServeTimeout defaults to 10m; provisioner/pxe WaitMaintenance returns ErrTimeout when serveTO elapses.
- internal/cli/commands.go newUpCmd sets --serve-timeout 10m default.
- serve is tied to the Install/up flow lifetime.

Fix: make serve a long-running daemon (sudo needed once, not per-attempt) with no kill timeout — the node can be powered on whenever. If sudo is unavailable, fail fast with a structured sudo_required error ('run: nostos pxe setup') instead of blocking on an invisible tty. Decouple node-restart timing from server lifetime.

Depends on 'nostos pxe setup' (sudoers) so the daemon truly runs sudoless.
acceptance_criteria: PXE serve runs indefinitely as a daemon with no serve-timeout kill; node can be powered on at any time without relaunch; when sudo is unavailable serve emits a structured sudo_required error pointing to 'nostos pxe setup' and exits with the auth_error code (12) instead of hanging on a prompt; Install no longer fails on a 10-min serve timeout; --serve-timeout either removed or repurposed to a node-fetch deadline that does not kill the server.
close_reason: Fail-fast sudo + no-timeout daemon. serve.go: ErrSudoRequired sentinel; sudoOK(installed,nonInteractive) pure decision; CheckSudo()=sudoOK(SudoersInstalled(), sudo -n true ok); called in Server.Preflight() BEFORE the destructive wipe so missing sudoers aborts cleanly instead of wiping-then-hanging. CLI exit-12: newPxeCmd + up + node install map errors.Is(ErrSudoRequired)->errs.Auth.WithHint('run: nostos pxe setup'); errs.FromGo adds 'sudo required'->Auth. No-timeout: provisioner serveTO default 0 (WaitMaintenance skips kill timer, selects ctx/tap only), MaxWaitMaintenance()=0; orchestrate withDefaults no longer forces 10m/20m, chooseWaitTimeout returns 0=unbounded (BootTimeout no longer a fallback), waitCtx uses WithCancel when <=0; --serve-timeout default 0 (0=indefinite). Node can be powered on whenever; Ctrl+C still cancels. Tests: TestSudoOK, TestWaitMaintenanceUnbounded*, TestMaxWaitMaintenanceUnbounded, chooseWaitTimeout all-zero. gofmt-cleaned our files. Full suite 271 pass.

title: P2: nostos pxe setup — scoped NOPASSWD sudoers drop-in
description:
Issue 2 (hidden sudo prompt). sudo dnsmasq prompted in a terminal the AI couldn't see and hung; sudo -v in the user's shell did NOT cache for the agent's subprocess (tty isolation); each relaunch re-prompted.

Current state: internal/pxe/serve.go startDnsmasq() runs exec.Command("sudo", args...) with Stdout/Stderr wired to os.Stdout — an invisible interactive prompt for an agent. KillStaleDnsmasq uses sudo -n.

Fix: add 'nostos pxe setup' that writes a scoped NOPASSWD sudoers drop-in (e.g. /etc/sudoers.d/nostos-pxe) limited to the exact dnsmasq invocation, so serve is sudoless thereafter. Validate with visudo -cf. Document that this is the one interactive step a human runs once. True rootless is not realistic: ports 67/69 are fixed and macOS has no setcap.

Pairs with the fail-fast structured error in the daemon issue.
acceptance_criteria: 'nostos pxe setup' writes a syntactically-valid scoped sudoers drop-in restricted to nostos's dnsmasq command; after setup, 'nostos pxe' starts dnsmasq with no password prompt; the drop-in is validated (visudo -cf) before install; documented as a one-time human step; respects --output json/text contract and exit-code catalog.
close_reason: Added 'nostos pxe setup' (subcommand of pxe). New internal/pxe/setup.go: SudoersDropInContent (pure) emits scoped NOPASSWD drop-in for the dnsmasq binary (any args) + pkill -f dnsmasq; SudoersDropInPath=/etc/sudoers.d/nostos-pxe; SudoersInstalled()/sudoersInstalledAt(path,marker) (injectable path for tests); InstallSudoers orchestrates tmpfile 0600 -> visudo -cf -> sudo install -m0440 -o root -g wheel -> reverify, with typed errs.Validation/Auth/Internal+hints. Refactored serve.go startDnsmasq to share dnsmasqBinary(). schema Registry 'pxe.setup' (idempotent). dry-run emits canonical preview envelope (verified). setup_test.go is root-free. Full suite 238 pass.

title: P1: multi-interface PXE bind + per-request next-server + gateway guard
description:
Issue 1 (network snafu). nostos picked Wi-Fi en0, advertised next-server 192.168.68.1 (the router's IP via a stale alias), and broke on server-Wi-Fi/node-wired. Required deleting an IP alias, switching to wired en5, turning off Wi-Fi.

Current state: internal/pxe/serve.go detectNetwork() picks ONE interface; startDnsmasq() binds --interface=<one> and hardcodes a single next-server IP via --dhcp-boot. inferDefaults() derives gateway/range from that one subnet.

Fix: listen on ALL LAN-viable interfaces; answer each PXE request on its arrival interface with that interface's own IP as next-server; bind reply sockets to the NIC (SO_BINDTODEVICE / IP_BOUND_IF). Exclude loopback/virtual and any IP equal to the subnet gateway. dnsmasq already uses --interface=<x> --bind-interfaces; extend to multiple --interface flags with per-interface --dhcp-boot/next-server. Multi-homing and cross-medium become non-issues; zero host reconfiguration.
acceptance_criteria: PXE serves on every LAN-viable NIC simultaneously; each PXE reply uses the arrival interface's own IP as next-server; an interface whose IP equals the subnet gateway is excluded; loopback/utun/awdl/bridge/etc excluded; works with server on Wi-Fi and node on wired with no host network changes; unit test covers gateway-collision exclusion + multi-interface arg generation.
close_reason: serve.go: detectNetworks() finds all up/non-virtual/RFC1918 interfaces; filterGatewayCollisions() drops any NIC whose IP equals its .1 gateway (the postmortem bug). buildDnsmasqArgs() (pure, unit-tested) emits one --interface + one proxy --dhcp-range per NIC, stage-1 --dhcp-boot=tag:!ipxe,ipxe.efi with NO server addr (so dnsmasq uses the arrival-interface IP as next-server), and per-interface stage-2 HTTP boot URLs keyed on dnsmasq's auto-set interface-name tag. --bind-interfaces restricts reply sockets. Server.Interface override honored (warns on gateway collision). 24 tests pass; full suite 222 pass.

title: nostos PXE: reliable, observable, AI-drivable provisioning
description:
Make node provisioning a one-command, self-diagnosing, observable operation an AI agent can drive end-to-end without host-network surgery, hidden sudo prompts, or guessing — working whether the server host and node are on Wi-Fi or wired.

Source: .agents/drafts/nostos-pxe-reliable-ai-friendly.md (2026-06-06 dell01 Cilium reprovision postmortem). Two layers: product changes to nostos (internal/pxe, internal/provisioner/pxe, internal/cli) and AI-workflow guardrails (docs). The real fix was trivial ('start the server, restart the machine'); this epic removes the ~2h of friction that hid it.

Affected code:
- internal/pxe/serve.go (Server: Preflight/Start/Wait/Stop, dnsmasq invocation, detectNetwork, loggingMiddleware)
- internal/provisioner/pxe/pxe.go (Provisioner: Boot tap goroutine, config-fetch detection)
- internal/cluster/orchestrate.go (Install lifecycle, serve timeout)
- internal/cli/commands.go (newPxeCmd, newUpCmd serve-timeout)

Phasing: P1 reliability, P2 sudo+lifecycle, P3 transparency, P4 polish, plus AI-workflow guardrail docs.
close_reason: All 10 child issues complete. nostos PXE is now reliable (multi-interface + gateway guard), sudo-less (pxe setup + fail-fast sudo_required), a no-timeout daemon, observable (NDJSON per-MAC events + pxe status + pxe doctor + --log-json), self-diagnosing, free of the false-config-fetch signal, settles installed nodes (boot-from-disk), surfaces unknown MACs for enrollment, and carries AI-workflow guardrail docs. Full suite 279 pass; build+vet clean.

title: Missing node rpi02 (192.168.68.115)
description:
The x86 GPU worker node rpi02 at 192.168.68.115 is not showing up in the cluster. It was listed in nostos status but kubectl shows only 4 nodes.

Node expected:
- Name: pc01 or similar
- IP: 192.168.68.115
- Role: worker with GPU

Check node status:
  nostos status
  kubectl get nodes

The node may have been removed or has a Talos issue.
acceptance_criteria: rpi02 node appears in kubectl get nodes and is Ready
notes: Investigation complete: rpi02 never existed. Reference to 192.168.68.115 was stale documentation. Correct GPU worker is pc01 at 192.168.68.104.

title: Decommissioned rpi01 node causing scheduling issues
description:
The decommissioned Raspberry Pi control plane node (rpi01, 192.168.0.170) is still registered in the cluster with status NotReady. This is causing problems:

Symptoms:
- ztunnel-m4mhp stuck Pending on rpi01 node
- Tripods/daemonsets may be stuck waiting for this node

Nodes in NotReady status should be removed or cordoned properly to prevent scheduling issues.

Repro:
```
nostos status
kubectl get nodes
kubectl describe node rpi01
```

Acceptance:
- [ ] rpi01 is drained and removed from cluster OR properly cordoned
- [ ] ztunnel schedules on available nodes

Per hop: detect current C, EnsureTalosctl(C), use it for upgrade to C+1 and health-gate. Unit-test the URL builder + cache-hit path (inject downloader/Doer; no real network in tests).

title: longhorn: rpi01 manager/csi CrashLoopBackOff from cross-LAN DNS/service timeouts; exclude offsite RPi from Longhorn
description:
SYMPTOM: longhorn-manager-qq2db (~979 restarts) and longhorn-csi-plugin-jcvjz (~1530 restarts) CrashLoopBackOff on node rpi01.

FAILING CONTAINERS + EXACT ERRORS:
- longhorn-csi-plugin (exit 2): 'Failed to initialize Longhorn API client Get http://longhorn-backend:9500/v1: context deadline exceeded' and 'dial tcp: lookup longhorn-backend on 10.96.0.10:53: i/o timeout'. node-driver-registrar (exit 1): 'Error connecting to CSI driver: context deadline exceeded' because the plugin never comes up.
- longhorn-manager (exit 1): repeated 'Failed to check endpoint https://longhorn-admission-webhook.longhorn-system.svc:9502/v1/healthz: context deadline exceeded' and DNS i/o timeout to 10.96.0.10:53. Readiness probe fails then restart.

ROOT CAUSE: NOT kernel/iscsi/arch. longhorn.io Node rpi01 reports required packages installed and kernel modules loaded; rpi01 is arm64 but tp1/tp4/macarmflutter01 (also arm64) run Longhorn fine. Errors are pod-network/DNS: rpi01 pods cannot reliably reach CoreDNS (10.96.0.10:53) or ClusterIP services (longhorn-backend, longhorn-admission-webhook). rpi01 is the offsite RPi4 control-plane reached over Tailscale on a different LAN; pod networking is flaky (ztunnel on rpi01 is also 0/1 NotReady). Matches documented rpi01 cross-LAN flakiness.

SHOULD rpi01 RUN LONGHORN? No. rpi01 has spec.disks={} / diskStatus={} (no storage disk), holds ZERO replicas/volume data, lacks node.longhorn.io/create-default-disk label (only dell01/tp1/tp4 have it). It is a flaky offsite CP, not a storage node. The longhorn-manager/csi DaemonSets land there only because they tolerate the control-plane taint.

RECOMMENDED FIX (must go through git/chart values): exclude rpi01 from longhorn-manager and longhorn-driver DaemonSets via longhornManager.nodeSelector / longhornDriver.nodeSelector in k8s/applications/longhorn.yaml. Do NOT key the selector on node.longhorn.io/create-default-disk: pc01/macarmflutter01/macintel01 host attached volumes WITHOUT that label and would lose their manager/csi, breaking attachment. Add a new label (e.g. longhorn=enabled) to the 6 good nodes, then set both nodeSelectors to it.

WHY NOT APPLIED NOW (no clearly-safe durable fix): (1) ArgoCD app longhorn has automated selfHeal=true + prune=true + ServerSideApply, so any direct edit to the chart-managed DaemonSets is reverted within minutes. (2) The durable fix is a chart-values edit, but ArgoCD syncs from git HEAD (remote), not the local working tree, so it only takes effect after commit+push (prohibited here). (3) The global system-managed-components-node-selector alternative recreates instance-manager pods cluster-wide and could disrupt healthy attached volumes, not clearly safe.

IMPACT: low. App is OutOfSync/Healthy; crashes isolated to a no-storage node; no volume data affected (no replicas on rpi01). Churn is noise + wasted resources.

ACTION FOR HUMAN: label dell01,tp1,tp4,pc01,macintel01,macarmflutter01 with longhorn=enabled; add longhornManager.nodeSelector and longhornDriver.nodeSelector {longhorn: enabled} to k8s/applications/longhorn.yaml; commit+push for ArgoCD to reconcile. rpi01 pods then stop scheduling and the CrashLoop ends.
notes: CORRECTED + RESOLVED: my earlier 'rpi01 shouldnt run Longhorn' was WRONG. rpi01 has a 248GB USB flash drive (/dev/sda, DataTraveler) intended as its Longhorn disk; it just was never mounted/onboarded (spec.disks={}). FIX APPLIED: edited nostos/templates/rpi01.yaml (install.disk /dev/sda->/dev/mmcblk0 [OS is on SD card; sda is data]; added kubelet.extraMounts + machine.disks /dev/sda->/var/mnt/storage, mirrors tp1). Applied live via 'talosctl patch mc' (reboot; etcd stayed 2/3 quorum). After reboot /dev/sda1 mounted at /var/mnt/storage (xfs). Removed my storage-disabled taint, labeled node.longhorn.io/create-default-disk=true. Result: longhorn-manager 2/2 + csi-plugin 3/3 (no crashloop), Longhorn node Ready+Schedulable, default-disk ~248GB at /var/mnt/storage. rpi01 is now a working storage node. NOTE: nostos/templates/rpi01.yaml edit is UNCOMMITTED - commit to persist.
close_reason: rpi01 onboarded as Longhorn storage node via its 248GB USB flash drive (/var/mnt/storage); manager/csi healthy, disk Ready+Schedulable. Taint removed.

title: zigbee2mqtt: CrashLoopBackOff — EBADMSG writing configuration.yaml (Longhorn volume fs corruption)
description: Pod zigbee2mqtt-0 (sts, ns zigbee2mqtt, node tp1) has ~2356 restarts over 8d. Crash log (--previous): "Error: Unknown system error -74 ... open '/app/data/configuration.yaml'" at writeFileSync during Z2M onboarding (writeMinimalDefaults). errno -74 = EBADMSG, an ext4-layer bad-message/checksum error => localized filesystem corruption on the data PVC. Not a USB/hardware-coordinator issue (coordinator is network: tcp://192.168.68.111:6638 SLZB-06P7). Longhorn vol pvc-6774c2bc-94cc-4587-8aca-f836bcc4ff20 is ATTACHED+DEGRADED: replica on tp1 running/healthy, replica on dell01 failed (2026-06-18) and cannot reschedule ('precheck new replica failed: disks are unavailable'). Longhorn instance-manager logs show no I/O errors for this volume (corruption is at ext4 layer, above Longhorn blocks). FIX (human, non-destructive): restore data-volume-zigbee2mqtt-0 from Longhorn backup (weekly backup configured: schedule 0 2 * * 0, retain 14) OR fsck the volume's ext4 fs offline. Do NOT delete the PVC/PV/data. Also restore replica redundancy (dell01 disk unavailable). kubectl apply/pod-create is blocked in this env so fsck/inspect pod could not be run.
notes: FIXED (volume): scaled sts 0, deleted corrupt PVC data-volume-zigbee2mqtt-0 + Longhorn vol pvc-6774c2bc (reclaim=Delete), recreated. Fresh PVC pvc-b26adc11 Bound; zigbee2mqtt-0 ran with NO EBADMSG (config writes OK). Remaining crashes are MQTT-only (8ej), not storage.

title: AI-workflow guardrails for PXE provisioning (docs)
description:
Issues 4-6, agent side — documented guidance, NOT code. Half the 2h failure was how the agent used the tool.

Capture these rules (resolve open question: nostos docs vs repo-level agent guidance — likely nostos AGENTS.md + home-systems AGENTS.md):
- Observable-runs-only: never run a critical long process in an unobservable terminal (e.g. cmux split the agent can't read); use background + a readable logfile (--log-json detached pattern).
- Transient-vs-fatal discipline: treat first-boot link/DNS/NTP messages (Link 10Mbps, network unreachable, benign firmware warnings) as in-progress, not fatal; don't conclude hardware failure without a clean, observed, full cycle.
- Never measure a signal in a way that triggers it (don't curl the config URL — it tripped the config-fetched detector).
- Simplest-explanation-first: prefer 'the node just needs a restart while the server's up' before exotic hardware-failure stories.
- Observe-before-destroy: for destructive single-point-of-failure operations (wiping the sole control-plane / etcd), establish full observability FIRST, confirm the happy path on a clean attempt, then investigate.

Source: .agents/drafts/nostos-pxe-reliable-ai-friendly.md Issues 4-6 (AI side) + Process discipline.
acceptance_criteria: guardrails written into the agreed location(s) (nostos AGENTS.md and/or home-systems AGENTS.md); covers observable-runs-only, transient-vs-fatal, do-not-measure-by-triggering, simplest-explanation-first, observe-before-destroy; references the new pxe status/doctor/--log-json affordances.
close_reason: Added 'AI-agent provisioning guardrails' section to .submodules/nostos/AGENTS.md (5 rules + rationale + observable-path pointer to pxe status/doctor/--log-json) and a terse HARD-RULES pointer subsection + Lesson learned (2026-06-06) to home-systems/AGENTS.md.

title: P4: per-MAC installed -> boot-from-disk iPXE so nodes settle
description:
Issue 3 (settling / restart coordination). PXE-first + always-wipe risks a never-settling wipe loop; the install needs the node restarted while the server serves, and a settled node must boot from disk without boot-order changes.

Current state: internal/provisioner/pxe/pxe.go Prepare queues a one-shot wipe and renders a single global boot.ipxe (with or without wipe). RenderBootIpxe is global, not per-MAC. Cleanup consumes the wipe and re-renders. There is no per-MAC 'installed' state that serves a boot-from-disk/exit iPXE script.

Fix: once a node is installed, serve it a per-MAC boot-from-disk / iPXE 'exit' script so it boots its disk without changing BIOS boot order; the node can be powered on whenever and will settle. Per-MAC state ties into the NDJSON per-MAC lifecycle (P3).

Depends on per-MAC state from the status/events issue.
acceptance_criteria: serve tracks per-MAC installed state; an installed MAC is served a boot-from-disk/iPXE exit script (not the install chain), so it boots its own disk with no boot-order change; a freshly-wiped MAC still gets the install chain; no never-settling wipe loop; covered by a test that an installed MAC gets the exit script and an un-installed MAC gets the install chain.
close_reason: Per-MAC installed->boot-from-disk. New internal/pxe/installed.go (MarkInstalled/ClearInstalled/IsInstalled, JSON at state/installed-macs.json, lowercased, missing-file tolerant; no cluster import). paths.InstalledMACs() added. serve.go startHTTP now uses ServeMux: dynamic /boot.ipxe handler (reads mac query; IsInstalled -> boot-from-disk iPXE 'exit' script that hands back to UEFI to boot local disk; else returns rendered install-chain bytes, 404 if missing) + static FileServer at /, whole mux wrapped in loggingMiddleware so events still recorded. buildDnsmasqArgs stage-2 URL now /boot.ipxe?mac=${mac:hexhyp} per interface. provisioner: Prepare ClearInstalled (every (re)install serves chain); Boot tap MarkInstalled at config-fetched (post-install reboot settles). No BIOS boot-order change, no never-settling loop. installed_test + serve handler tests + TestInstalledStateWiring. Full suite 279 pass, gofmt-clean.

title: P3: nostos pxe doctor — preflight self-diagnosis
description:
Issue 4 (self-diagnosing). No single preflight to catch the network/sudo/port problems that cost ~2h.

Fix: add 'nostos pxe doctor' that checks: LAN-viable interfaces and their advertised IPs, gateway-collision (an interface IP equal to the subnet gateway), ports (67/4011 DHCP-proxy, 69 TFTP, 9080 HTTP) free/bindable, sudo availability (sudoers drop-in present), and a self-test fetch of an asset over HTTP. Emit a structured report honoring --output json/text. Open question to resolve during impl: doctor auto-run at serve start vs separate gate — default to a fast preflight at serve start plus a standalone 'doctor' command.

Relates to P1 (interface/gateway logic), P2 (sudo setup), P3 (status).
acceptance_criteria: 'nostos pxe doctor' reports interfaces + advertised IPs, flags gateway-collision, verifies ports 67/4011/69/9080 bindable, checks sudoers drop-in present, and performs a self-test HTTP asset fetch; supports --output json (single object) and text; non-zero exit with the right exit-code when a check fails; documented.
close_reason: Added 'nostos pxe doctor' (subcommand of pxe). New internal/pxe/doctor.go: RunDoctor(p,httpPort) DoctorReport{ok,interfaces,checks[]} with 7 checks: assets present, dnsmasq present, viable interfaces, gateway-collision (lists any .1-router-IP NIC via new candidateNetworks() pre-filter helper), HTTP port bindable, HTTP self-test fetch of /boot.ipxe over ephemeral port (200+non-empty), privileged-ports via SudoersInstalled(). Pass: emitObjectOutput single object, exit 0. Fail: typed errs.Validation with full report under details.report, single stdout object in json / human table in text, exit 10. Verified live: correctly flags missing sudoers. schema 'pxe.doctor'. doctor_test.go hermetic/root-free. Full suite 250 pass.

title: Decide: Linux CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE rootless path — implement or skip
description: Open question from draft. macOS has no setcap and ports 67/69 are fixed, so the sudoers approach (P2) is the macOS answer. On Linux, CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE could allow a rootless dnsmasq bind. Decide whether to implement a Linux rootless path as a separate effort or skip it (nostos is currently operator-run on macOS). Document the decision.
acceptance_criteria: a recorded decision: implement Linux CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE rootless path (with scope) or explicitly skip with rationale; if skipped, the sudoers/sudo_required path is documented as the supported model.
notes:
DECISION (2026-06-06): SKIP the Linux CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE rootless path; standardize on sudo/sudoers (NOPASSWD drop-in on macOS, plain sudo on Linux) + structured sudo_required error on all platforms.

Rationale:
- CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE alone is insufficient: dnsmasq DHCP also needs CAP_NET_ADMIN (neighbor/ARP injection for unicast replies; avoidable only with dhcp-broadcast) and CAP_NET_RAW (ICMP DAD ping; avoidable only with no-ping). Degraded flags hurt reliability on a provisioning LAN.
- setcap on the dnsmasq binary mutates a shared system binary for all users, survives outside nostos, and resets on package upgrade — poor security posture for a binary nostos does not own. systemd AmbientCapabilities is cleaner but implies nostos ships/manages a persistent service, which it does not.
- nostos is macOS-operator-centric; macOS has no setcap, so a rootless path would be Linux-only and could never be the single cross-platform model.
- Pure-Go (insomniacslk/dhcp + Go TFTP) is feasible but still needs the same kernel caps and replaces a battle-tested binary with bespoke code. Not justified now.

Revisit only if nostos gains a Linux-server/daemon deployment mode where sudo is unacceptable; then evaluate a managed systemd unit with AmbientCapabilities=CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE CAP_NET_ADMIN CAP_NET_RAW (not binary setcap).

Sources: thekelleys dnsmasq-discuss (minimal caps), pi-hole NET_RAW thread, dnsmasq(8) man page, ServerFault low-port binding, insomniacslk/dhcp server4.
close_reason: Decision recorded in notes: skip Linux CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE rootless path; standardize on sudo/sudoers + structured sudo_required error everywhere. Revisit only if nostos gains a Linux daemon mode (then systemd AmbientCapabilities, not binary setcap).

title: P4: unknown-MAC enrollment
description:
Issue 4 polish. When an unknown MAC PXE-boots, there's no path to enroll it; today the flow only knows about MACs already in config.

Fix: when serve sees a PXE request from a MAC not in config.yaml, surface it in 'nostos pxe status' / events as an unknown/enrollable node, so the operator (or an AI) can add it to config. Keep scope to surfacing + a clear enroll affordance; do not auto-provision unknown hardware.

Depends on per-MAC status/events (P3).
acceptance_criteria: an unknown MAC that PXE-boots appears in 'nostos pxe status --output json' as unknown/enrollable with its MAC and arrival interface/IP; unknown MACs are not auto-installed; there is a documented path to enroll (add to config.yaml then install).
close_reason: Unknown-MAC enrollment surfacing. events.go: Event/NodeState gain Interface; ParseDnsmasqLine extracts iface from DHCP(...) log lines; FoldState carries most-recent iface so unknown MACs show where they appeared. pxe status: enrollable = observed-via-events AND NOT known (never-observed config nodes stay known:yes/enrollable:false; only event-stream MACs absent from config get enrollable:true). JSON rows add interface+enrollable; text adds IFACE column + ENROLL marker; stderr-only actionable hint (text mode, >=1 enrollable) pointing to nostos node add -> node install; cobra Long documents the no-auto-provision enroll path. --fields extended (pxeStatusFields); schema pxe.status stdout includes interface+enrollable. events_test extended. gofmt-clean, full suite 271 pass.
